previous_statement: (GNS decomposition for measures). For a probability measure μ on X with finite support {x_1, ..., x_k} (i.e., μ = Σ_{j=1}^k λ_j δ_{x_j} with λ_j > 0 and Σ λ_j = 1), the GNS representation is π_μ ≅ ⊕_{j=1}^k ev_{x_j}^{⊕1}, with commutant π_μ(C(X))' ≅ ⊕_{j=1}^k C ≅ C^k. Hence dim_R(π_μ(C(X))'^{sa}) = k (each summand contributes n_j² = 1² = 1).
new_statement:
(GNS decomposition for states on C(X): finite-dimensional commutant implies finite atomic support, and explicit GNS computation). Let A = C(X) for X compact Hausdorff. For a state phi_mu on C(X) corresponding to a Borel probability measure mu on X (via Node 1.5.1), we establish two results:

(I) FINITE COMMUTANT DIMENSION IMPLIES FINITE ATOMIC SUPPORT: If dim_R(pi_mu(C(X))'^{sa}) < infinity, then mu is atomic with finite support. Proof: The GNS commutant pi_mu(C(X))' is isomorphic (as a von Neumann algebra) to L^infty(X, mu) acting by multiplication on L^2(X, mu). The algebra L^infty(X, mu) is finite-dimensional if and only if mu is a finite sum of point masses (atoms). Forward direction: if L^infty(X, mu) is finite-dimensional, say of dimension k, then L^2(X, mu) is k-dimensional, which forces mu to be supported on exactly k atoms (the indicator functions of the atoms form a basis). Backward direction: if mu = sum_{j=1}^k lambda_j delta_{x_j} with distinct x_j and lambda_j > 0, then L^infty(X, mu) = C^k (functions on a k-point set). Thus: dim_R(pi_mu(C(X))'^{sa}) < infinity implies mu = sum_{j=1}^k lambda_j delta_{x_j} for some k < infinity with distinct points x_1, ..., x_k in X and weights lambda_j > 0 summing to 1.

(II) EXPLICIT GNS DECOMPOSITION FOR FINITELY-SUPPORTED MEASURES: For mu = sum_{j=1}^k lambda_j delta_{x_j} with distinct points x_1, ..., x_k in X and lambda_j > 0, the GNS representation is pi_mu = direct_sum_{j=1}^k ev_{x_j} (each evaluation representation appearing with multiplicity 1), and the commutant is pi_mu(C(X))' = C^k. Hence dim_R(pi_mu(C(X))'^{sa}) = k (each summand contributes n_j^2 = 1^2 = 1, where n_j = 1 is the multiplicity of the j-th evaluation representation).

Proof of (II): The GNS Hilbert space is H_mu = completion of C(X)/N_mu where N_mu = {f : phi_mu(f*f) = 0} = {f : sum lambda_j |f(x_j)|^2 = 0} = {f : f(x_j) = 0 for all j = 1,...,k}. Thus C(X)/N_mu is isomorphic to C^k via f -> (f(x_1), ..., f(x_k)), and H_mu = C^k (already complete since finite-dimensional). The inner product is the weighted one: <f, g>_mu = sum lambda_j f(x_j) conj(g(x_j)). The GNS representation acts by multiplication: pi_mu(f) = diag(f(x_1), ..., f(x_k)). By Urysohn's lemma (X compact Hausdorff implies X normal, and {x_1,...,x_k} is finite hence closed), for any values (alpha_1,...,alpha_k) in C^k there exists g in C(X) with g(x_j) = alpha_j, so pi_mu(C(X)) is the FULL diagonal subalgebra of M_k(C). The unitary U : (C^k, weighted) -> (C^k, standard) defined by U(e_j) = sqrt(lambda_j) e_j intertwines pi_mu with direct_sum_{j=1}^k ev_{x_j}, verifying the decomposition. The commutant of the full diagonal subalgebra in M_k(C) is the diagonal subalgebra itself, so pi_mu(C(X))' = C^k (abelian, confirming n_j = 1 for all j by Node 1.5.2).

This is a deduction depending on Nodes 1.5.1 (states = probability measures), 1.5.2 (all multiplicities n_j = 1 for abelian case), the GNS construction, and Urysohn's lemma (X compact Hausdorff implies normal, hence continuous functions separate finite point sets).
